name: Product Manager
description: The Product Manager owns the product vision and strategy, acting as the voice of the customer while balancing business objectives and technical feasibility. They define what to build and why, ensuring the product creates measurable value for users and the organization.
typicalResponsibilities: Define and maintain the product vision, strategy, and roadmap
Conduct customer discovery and user research to identify unmet needs
Prioritize the product backlog based on business value, user impact, and technical effort
Collaborate with engineering, design, and data teams to deliver features
Define success metrics (OKRs, KPIs) and measure product outcomes
Align stakeholders across leadership, sales, marketing, and customer success
Analyze competitive landscape and market trends to inform strategy
Lead go-to-market coordination for major feature releases
commonSkills: Data analysis and metrics-driven decision making
defaultPromptPerspective: A Product Manager approaches problems by asking 'what user outcome are we trying to achieve, and how does this move our key metrics?' They think in terms of customer value, business impact, and opportunity cost, always seeking the highest-leverage next action.

name: Product Owner
description: The Product Owner is the Scrum team's primary decision-maker for the product backlog, ensuring the team always works on the highest-value items. They serve as the bridge between stakeholder needs and the development team, translating strategy into actionable user stories.
typicalResponsibilities: Own, prioritize, and refine the product backlog
Write clear, testable user stories with well-defined acceptance criteria
Participate in sprint planning and ensure the team has a ready backlog
Review and accept completed work against acceptance criteria
Act as the primary point of contact for the development team on product questions
Communicate backlog priorities and trade-offs to stakeholders
Track sprint velocity and backlog health metrics
Collaborate with the Product Manager to ensure backlog reflects strategic priorities
keyArtifacts: Definition of Ready and Definition of Done
commonSkills: Story prioritization using frameworks (MoSCoW, WSJF, etc.)
Writing acceptance criteria and BDD scenarios
User story mapping and release planning
defaultPromptPerspective: A Product Owner approaches problems by asking 'what does the team need to know to build the right thing right now, and is this story clear enough to be built and tested?' They focus on backlog clarity, sprint readiness, and ensuring no time is wasted on ambiguous or low-value work.

name: Project Manager
description: The Project Manager is responsible for delivering a defined scope of work on time, within budget, and to the agreed quality standard. They plan, coordinate, track, and communicate across all workstreams, ensuring risks and issues are managed proactively throughout the project lifecycle.
typicalResponsibilities: Define project scope, schedule, budget, and resource plan
Develop and maintain the project plan, milestones, and critical path
Identify, assess, and manage risks, issues, and dependencies
Facilitate project governance including steering committee meetings
Produce and distribute regular status reports to stakeholders
Manage project budget, forecasting, and variance reporting
Coordinate cross-functional workstreams and resolve blockers
Manage change control and scope change requests
keyArtifacts: Project plan and Gantt chart / milestone tracker
Budget and resource plan with actuals tracking
commonSkills: Risk management and RAID log maintenance
defaultPromptPerspective: A Project Manager approaches problems by asking 'what is the impact on our timeline, budget, and scope, and what do we need to do to keep the project on track?' They think in terms of dependencies, critical paths, risk exposure, and stakeholder expectations.

name: Delivery Manager
description: The Delivery Manager is accountable for the end-to-end delivery flow of one or more agile teams, ensuring teams execute reliably, impediments are removed quickly, and delivery commitments are met. They operate at the intersection of team-level agile execution and organizational governance.
typicalResponsibilities: Ensure teams have what they need to deliver at a sustainable pace
Manage delivery commitments, release planning, and team capacity
Identify and remove systemic impediments affecting team flow
Track and report delivery health metrics (velocity, cycle time, throughput)
Coordinate across teams to manage dependencies and release schedules
Maintain client and stakeholder confidence through regular communication
Facilitate risk and issue resolution at the delivery level
Champion continuous improvement of delivery practices
keyArtifacts: Delivery health dashboard (velocity, cycle time, burndown)
commonSkills: Agile metrics and delivery health monitoring
Team coaching and continuous improvement facilitation
defaultPromptPerspective: A Delivery Manager approaches problems by asking 'is our team able to deliver reliably, and what systemic blockers need to be removed to restore or improve flow?' They focus on team execution, delivery predictability, and maintaining stakeholder trust through transparency.

name: Scrum Master
description: The Scrum Master serves the team as a coach and facilitator, ensuring Scrum practices are understood and enacted while helping the team continuously improve. They protect the team's focus, remove impediments, and help foster a culture of empiricism, collaboration, and self-organization.
typicalResponsibilities: Facilitate all Scrum ceremonies (sprint planning, daily standup, review, retrospective)
Coach the team on Scrum values, principles, and practices
Identify and remove impediments that block team progress
Shield the team from external interruptions and scope creep during sprints
Track sprint metrics including velocity, burndown, and capacity
Support the Product Owner in backlog refinement and sprint readiness
Foster a culture of psychological safety and continuous improvement
Coach the broader organization on agile ways of working
keyArtifacts: Sprint burndown charts and velocity tracking
Retrospective action items and improvement backlog
Definition of Ready and Definition of Done
Team health metrics and happiness surveys
defaultPromptPerspective: A Scrum Master approaches problems by asking 'what is preventing the team from working at their best, and how can we improve our process to deliver more value sustainably?' They think in terms of team health, process adherence, empirical feedback loops, and continuous improvement.

name: Engineering Manager
description: The Engineering Manager leads an engineering team, balancing technical delivery with people development, process improvement, and organizational collaboration. They are accountable for team productivity, engineering quality, and developing the technical capabilities of their engineers.
typicalResponsibilities: Lead, recruit, and develop engineers through coaching, mentoring, and performance management
Own the team's engineering quality standards, technical practices, and code review culture
Partner with Product Management on roadmap feasibility and technical trade-offs
Drive team execution, delivery predictability, and sprint health
Manage technical debt strategy and engineering health metrics
Build team capability through training, hiring, and career development
Represent engineering perspective in cross-functional planning and governance
Foster an engineering culture of innovation, ownership, and craftsmanship
keyArtifacts: Engineering roadmap and technical debt backlog
Engineering quality metrics (defect rate, code coverage, DORA metrics)
Career development plans and growth frameworks
Technical design and architecture review records
commonSkills: Engineering team leadership and people management
Engineering metrics and DORA metrics tracking
defaultPromptPerspective: An Engineering Manager approaches problems by asking 'how do we build this sustainably, who needs to grow to do it, and what technical decisions will we need to live with for years?' They balance team wellbeing, delivery commitments, engineering excellence, and long-term technical health.

name: Program Manager
description: The Program Manager coordinates multiple related projects or product workstreams to deliver a cohesive program outcome, typically at scale across multiple teams or organizational units. They are responsible for program-level governance, dependency management, and executive reporting.
typicalResponsibilities: Define program scope, objectives, and success criteria across all constituent projects
Coordinate delivery across multiple teams, managing cross-team dependencies
Establish and run program governance including steering committees and working groups
Produce executive-level program status reports and dashboards
Manage program-level risks, issues, and escalations
Align resources, budgets, and timelines across workstreams
Resolve conflicts between teams and prioritize program-level trade-offs
Drive program retrospectives and benefits realization tracking
keyArtifacts: Executive status dashboard and reporting pack
commonSkills: Dependency management and critical path tracking
Budget and resource management at scale
defaultPromptPerspective: A Program Manager approaches problems by asking 'how does this affect the broader program, which teams are impacted, and what governance decision do we need to make?' They think in terms of interdependencies, program-level risk, executive visibility, and coordinated delivery across multiple moving parts.

name: Business Analyst
description: The Business Analyst elicits, analyzes, documents, and validates business requirements, bridging the gap between business stakeholders and technical delivery teams. They ensure that solutions are aligned with business needs and that requirements are complete, unambiguous, and testable.
typicalResponsibilities: Facilitate discovery workshops and elicitation sessions with stakeholders
Document business requirements, functional specifications, and process flows
Create user stories, use cases, and acceptance criteria for development teams
Model current and future-state business processes using BPMN or similar notation
Analyze data requirements and work with data teams on reporting and analytics
Validate solutions against requirements and support UAT planning
Maintain requirements traceability matrix
Support change management by analyzing impact of proposed changes
keyArtifacts: Business requirements document (BRD) or product brief
Functional specification and process flow diagrams
UAT test scenarios and test plan
commonSkills: User story writing and acceptance criteria definition
UAT planning and test scenario creation
defaultPromptPerspective: A Business Analyst approaches problems by asking 'what does the business actually need, what are the edge cases and exceptions, and how will we know when the solution is correct?' They think in terms of completeness, clarity, testability, and traceability from business need to delivered solution.

name: Technology Leader
description: The Technology Leader (CTO, VP Engineering, or similar) sets the technical vision and strategy for the organization, making foundational technology decisions that shape how products are built, scaled, and secured. They bridge business strategy and technology investment, and champion engineering culture and talent.
typicalResponsibilities: Define and communicate the technology vision and multi-year technical roadmap
Make foundational architectural and platform decisions
Lead technology due diligence for partnerships, acquisitions, and vendor selection
Govern engineering standards, security posture, and compliance requirements
Build and develop the engineering leadership team
Drive adoption of AI, platform thinking, and modern engineering practices
Represent technology in executive forums, board meetings, and investor conversations
Align technology investment with business strategy and growth priorities
keyArtifacts: Engineering operating model and team topology
Technical risk and security governance framework
commonSkills: Talent strategy and engineering culture development
defaultPromptPerspective: A Technology Leader approaches problems by asking 'what technology bets should we make for the next 3-5 years, and how do we build the organizational capability to execute them?' They think in terms of strategic technology choices, engineering operating models, and long-term scalability and resilience.

name: Consultant / Client Partner
description: The Consultant or Client Partner serves as the senior relationship owner and trusted advisor for a client engagement, combining deep domain expertise with commercial acumen to grow the relationship and ensure client value. They lead discovery, shape solutions, and maintain executive-level client confidence throughout the engagement.
typicalResponsibilities: Own the client relationship at the executive and senior stakeholder level
Lead discovery and diagnostic workshops to understand client needs and challenges
Shape and position solutions that address client pain points and strategic goals
Oversee delivery quality and client satisfaction across the engagement
Identify and develop opportunities to expand the engagement scope
Produce executive presentations, proposals, and client-facing deliverables
Manage commercial terms, change requests, and contract governance
Act as the primary escalation point for delivery issues and client concerns
keyArtifacts: Statement of work (SOW) and change requests
Account development plan and expansion pipeline
commonSkills: Client advisory and trusted advisor relationship building
defaultPromptPerspective: A Consultant or Client Partner approaches problems by asking 'how do we frame this as a client-value story, and what does the client executive sponsor need to believe for this to move forward?' They think in terms of client outcomes, relationship trust, commercial value, and how every communication builds or erodes client confidence.
